coredata.dc:description: UV-induced photo-oxidation critically compromises food quality, necessitating advanced materials that simultaneously mitigate light degradation and enable real-time freshness monitoring. Here, the excellent UV-blocking films with sensitive humidity and pH response are developed by synergistically incorporating sodium ligninsulfonate-derived carbon quantum dots (L-CDs) via a simple microwave-assisted generation and light absorption of lignin from lignocellulosic nanofibers. The resulting films with hybrid structure can block 99.74 % (UV-C), 99.86 % (UV-B), and 87.90 % (UV-A) of radiation while maintaining 85.60 % visible light transmittance (600 nm), and has excellent mechanical strength (61.51 MPa). Under the protection of composite film, pork, strawberries and grapes all maintained longer freshness than unprotected controls. Profited from the protonation and deprotonation of L-CDs and the disconnection/reconstruction of hydrogen bonds, it shows sensitive humidity and pH response with changes in blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the conductivity of the composite film increases exponentially as the increase of humidity, showing excellent humidity response monitoring. As expected, the prepared composite film can detect changes in conductivity and resistance for monitoring the freshness of food. So, this work provides the development prospects for multi-functional composite films with UV-blocking and intelligent humidity & pH sensing for food preservation and intelligent monitoring.

head.ce:title: Sustainable lignocellulosic nanofibers-based films with sensitive humidity and pH response for UV-blocking food preservation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials
School of Resources, Environment and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, and School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xinyu Shao and Yanhui Xie contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: UV-induced photo-oxidation critically compromises food quality, necessitating advanced materials that simultaneously mitigate light degradation and enable real-time freshness monitoring. Here, the excellent UV-blocking films with sensitive humidity and pH response are developed by synergistically incorporating sodium ligninsulfonate-derived carbon quantum dots (L-CDs) via a simple microwave-assisted generation and light absorption of lignin from lignocellulosic nanofibers. The resulting films with hybrid structure can block 99.74 % (UV-C), 99.86 % (UV-B), and 87.90 % (UV-A) of radiation while maintaining 85.60 % visible light transmittance (600 nm), and has excellent mechanical strength (61.51 MPa). Under the protection of composite film, pork, strawberries and grapes all maintained longer freshness than unprotected controls. Profited from the protonation and deprotonation of L-CDs and the disconnection/reconstruction of hydrogen bonds, it shows sensitive humidity and pH response with changes in blue fluorescence. Furthermore, the conductivity of the composite film increases exponentially as the increase of humidity, showing excellent humidity response monitoring. As expected, the prepared composite film can detect changes in conductivity and resistance for monitoring the freshness of food. So, this work provides the development prospects for multi-functional composite films with UV-blocking and intelligent humidity & pH sensing for food preservation and intelligent monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photo-oxidation is an important factor that causes most meat, seafood, fruits, vegetables and dairy products to rot. Photo-oxidation results in the photochemical changes of food nutrients and the reduction of visual taste, and more seriously, a large number of harmful substances are produced. Ultraviolet radiation, especially UV-A (315–400 nm) and UV-B (280–315 nm) wavelengths, is the main culprit in food photo-oxidation. The use of organic nanomaterials (nanoencapsulated lycopene, nanoencapsulated β-carotene, carbon dots) [1,2] or inorganic nanomaterials (metal-organic frameworks, metals and/or metal oxides) [3–6] to prepare UV-blocking films is increasingly used in food preservation. However, traditional UV-blocking films cannot fulfill the existing demands of smart food packaging materials owing to the intrinsic constraints of ultraviolet blocking and visible light transmission, and the lack or singularity of intelligent response function. The composite biopolymer film has both UV-blocking and intelligent monitoring of food spoilage, which can prevent food photochemical oxidation over a brief duration as well as intelligent monitoring to guide food shelf life management [7,8]. Therefore, improving the versatility of biopolymer composite film is the key to the development of environmentally friendly and intelligent UV-blocking materials.

Lignocellulosic biopolymers, particularly cellulose and lignin [9], have been widely used in food protection and intelligent monitoring (pressure, humidity, temperature, pH) with unparalleled advantages as renewable, UV-absorbent substrates for advanced packaging [10]. And their rich chemical functional groups and unique properties (environmental friendliness, good UV-blocking) are of great significance [11,12]. Due to the function of cellulose based nanomaterials to scatter and absorb ultraviolet rays, the ultraviolet screening performance of biopolymer based films was advanced after adding cellulose nanofiber whisker [13]. Nanocellulose fiber as a composite film-forming substrate shows the advantages of the simplification of process and the reduction of cost [14]. Compared with nanocellulose, lignocellulosic nanofibers (LCNFs), derived directly from unbleached biomass, combine the mechanical robustness of cellulose with lignin's innate UV-blocking properties while circumventing energy-intensive processing [15]. In addition, LCNFs exhibit similar size and structural properties to CNFs, and the addition of lignin provides an important prerequisite for multifunctional LCNFs [16]. It was found that compared with CNFs, LCNFs successfully increased the tensile strength of polystyrene composite film by 30 %. The residual lignin of 5–14 wt% was beneficial to improve the heat resistance of the composite film. The composite film prepared by incorporating 10 % colloidal lignin particles to CNFs has the functions of waterproof, anti-oxidation and anti-ultraviolet [17,18]. Also, lignin exhibits inherent UV absorption attributes attributable to the existence of specific structural groups, for example phenolic moieties, carbonyl ketones, and other UV chromophoric entities [19,20]. Given its renewable attributes and promising performance, it is regarded as a superior alternative to conventional synthetic UV protection agents. Nonetheless, the inherently dark brown-yellow color of lignin and lignin derivatives obtained by extraction limits the realization of their high transparency films. Although a variety of UV-blocking films have been developed, such as nano lignin/PVA composite films, lignin/cellulose composite films, xylan/TiO2-PVA films, carbon quantum dots/cellulose films, and cellulose nanocrystalline composite films [21–25]. These films encounter obstacles for instance, low visible light transmittance, intricate production techniques, as well as the challenge of achieving multifunctional intelligence. So, for the monitoring of complex conditions and the application of different scenarios, traditional cellulose/lignin films have single functional properties and are not enough to meet complex requirements. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with quantum confinement effect, good water dispersion and photoluminescence, have potential applications in UV blocking field [26]. Lignin is rich in oxygenating groups and π-π conjugation, and has the potential to be the precursor of countless state-of-the-art carbon nanomaterials [27]. At present, lignin-derived CDs have been developed to explore their applicability in anti-counterfeiting, luminescence, drug tracking, cell monitoring, bioimaging, energy storage and conversion, etc. [28–30]. Thus, the integrated development of lignin-derived CDs and lignin co-optimized nanocellulose composite films has great potential in enhancing UV-blocking and multi-directional intelligent response monitoring.

Herein, we synthesized lignin-derived CDs through a simple and rapid one-step microwave-assisted process using sodium lignosulfonate with high carbon content and rich oxygen-containing functional groups as the precursor. The multi-functional composite films with UV-blocking and humidity & pH response were constructed by embedding L-CDs into the lignocellulosic nanofibers matrix. Lignin as a UV absorbing and shielding component cooperated with L-CDs to enhance the UV-blocking qualities of the composite films. Unlike prior lignin-cellulose composites, this design achieves exceptional UV-blocking (99.74 % UV-C, 99.86 % UV-B, 87.90 % UV-A) without sacrificing transparency (85.60 % at 600 nm), overcoming the opacity barrier of lignin-based materials. Furthermore, L-CDs have a large surface-volume ratio responsible for high humidity sensing. The adsorbed water molecules at the surface of the composite film form hydrogen bonds with the oxygen-rich reactive groups at the surface of L-CDs, thus promoting the movement of electrons between H2O molecules and CDs. Beneficially, the composite film showed good intelligent responsiveness in different humidity and pH environments. It was found that the conductivity of the composite film increased exponentially as the increase of ambient humidity. This work pioneers a sustainable platform for next-generation smart packaging that simultaneously mitigates photo-oxidation and monitors food freshness through multiple environmental cues.

Sodium Ligninsulfonate (SL, C20H24Na2O10S2, molecular weight: Mw = 534.5 Da) was provided from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Lignocellulosic nanofibers gel (LCNFs, carboxyl group content of 1.6–2.2 mmol/g, fiber length of 0.5–2.0 μm, mass fraction of 1.16 wt%) with different lignin contents (0 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 %) were provided by Tianjin Woodelf Biotechnology Co., LTD. Boric acid (H3BO3, MW = 61.83 Da, AR) was provided from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Co., LTD. Urea (H2NCONH2, MW = 60.06 Da, AR) was obtained from Tianjin Beichen Founder reagent factory. Fresh strawberries, pork and grapes were obtained from a local supermarket (Nanning, China). All raw materials and reagents were directly used in subsequent experiments without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A simple microwave-assisted generation of sodium ligninsulfonate-derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Add 0.045 g of SL, 0.3 g of boric acid and 0.3 g of urea into 20 mL of deionized water for stirring thoroughly to dissolve. Subsequently, the resultant mixture was conveyed to a microwave oven and heated for 190 s under high fire conditions at 100 °C to obtain a dark brown solid product. Add 10 mL of ultra-pure water into the upper cooled solid and stir thoroughly to make it dissolved completely. After that, the obtained solution was filtered with microporous membrane (0.22 μm) for further purification to obtain sodium ligninsulfonate-derived carbon quantum dots (L-CDs). Then, the L-CDs stock solution was diluted to different multiples (5 D, 10 D, 20 D, 30 D, 40 D) and placed for reserve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A green strategy for constructing humidity & pH responsive lignocellulosic nanofibers composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Add 8 mL of L-CDs stock solution with different dilution ratios (5 D, 10 D, 20 D, 30 D, 40 D) to 12 g LCNFs gel with lignin content of 5 %, stirred at 25 °C for 12 h, and then pour the solution into a petri dish with a diameter of 5 cm and dry at 35 °C for the formation of composite films to explore the optimal dilution ratio. Further, the effects of the added volumes (6 mL, 8 mL, 10 mL) of L-CDs solution under the optimal dilution ratio, the added masses (0.03 g, 0.045 g, 0.06 g, 0.09 g, 0.12 g) of SL for generation of L-CDs under the optimal volumes and dilution ratio of L-CDs solution were fabricated to evaluate the comprehensive properties of composite films. Deservedly, the influences of lignin contents (0 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 %) in lignocellulosic nanofibers gel on the performance parameters of the L-CDs/LCNFs composite films were also researched based on the above optimized experiments.

The fluorescence spectra of distinct composite films at the excitation wavelength of 385 nm–600 nm were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, Tianjin Gangdong Technology Co., Ltd. China). The optical transmittance and ultraviolet absorption of different composite films were defined by an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600Plus, Shimadzu corporation, Japan) ranging from 200 to 600 nm. The percentage of UV-blocking for UV-A (315–400 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-C (200–280 nm) was calculated using Eqs. (1), (2) and (3).

The colorimetric analysis of different composite films was determined by using a colorimeter (ADCI-60-C, Beijing Chentek Instrument Technology Co., LTD, China). The mechanical properties of different composite films with length of 15 mm and width of 2 mm were tested repeated for at least three times by using a universal testing machine (WDW-O5E, Jinan Lanbotes Instrument Co., LTD, China) with tensile speed of 1 mm/min. The micromorphology of CNFs film, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/CNFs film were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S-3400N, Hitachi corporation, Japan) with a voltage of 5 KV. The FTIR spectra of SL, L-CDs, CNFs film, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/CNFs film were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, IRTracer-100, Shimadzu corporation, Japan) at ATR mode with 32 scans. The XRD patterns of CNFs film, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/CNFs film were tested using the X-ray diffractometer (A24A10, BRUKER AXS GMBH, Germany). The Zeta potential of L-CDs, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/CNFs film were defined using a Zeta potential analyzer (NanoBrook Omni, Brookhaven, USA). The chemical functional group and molecular interaction analysis of L-CDs/LCNFs film was measured by using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The contact angles of CNFs film, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/CNFs film were tested using a contact angle meter (JCY-4, Shanghai Fangrui Instrument Co., LTD, China), repeated at least 5 times. The four-point probe method of a digital multimeter (IM3536, HIOKI corporation, Japan) was used to test the conductivity and thin layer resistance of L-CDs/CNFs film, repeated at least 5 times. For application of L-CDs/LCNFs film to food preservation and intelligent monitoring, fresh strawberries, pork and grapes coated with and without L-CDs/LCNFs film were exposed to UV light (ZGD-GHD, Zhongshan Xingxuzi Lighting Co., Ltd. China) for 36 h at 25 °C and observed the color change. Further, the conductivity and thin layer resistance were measured to monitor the deterioration process of the meat. The pork samples were homogenized using a blender (AQ-180E, CiXi NaiOu Trading Co., Ltd., China), followed by centrifugation (TG16-WS, Hunan Xiangxin Instrument and Meter Co., Ltd., China) to isolate the supernatant. Subsequently, the pH values of the supernatants were quantitatively determined using a calibrated digital desktop pH meter (PHS-3C, Shanghai Puchun Measurement Instrument Co., Ltd., China) following standard analytical protocols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of L-CDs and L-CDs/LCNFs composite film
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 illustrates the green strategy for assembling L-CDs/LCNFs composite film. To develop a unique green CDs (L-CDs) with UV-blocking and humidity & pH response, SL served as the carbon source, while urea acted as the nitrogen source, which was easily and quickly generated by microwave synthesis (Fig. 1a). SL is different from cellulose and hemicellulose in that it has UV chromophobe groups including quinone and methoxy-substituted phenoxy, and exhibits good UV absorption in the 250–400 nm range. The resulting L-CDs aqueous dispersion shows blue-green fluorescence at 365 nm (Fig. 1b). The absorbance spectrum of L-CDs solution exhibits a shoulder peak at 273 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC from the phenylcyclopropane structure of SL (Fig. 1b) [31,32]. To verify the chemical functional groups from L-CDs, FTIR spectrum was analyzed (Fig. 1c). It is evident that the broad peak at 3347 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH, and the peaks at 1670 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO bonds, proving the existence of carboxyl groups in L-CDs [33]. The absorption peak observed at 1620 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CC bonds. The peaks at 1470 cm−1, 1321 cm−1, and 775 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CN, CO, and CH bonds, specifically. In addition, the absorption band around 702 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of CS bonds [34]. The above analysis proves the successfully fabricated of L-CDs. For utilizing the UV-absorptive optical characteristics of L-CDs, the L-CDs/LCNFs films were fabricated through a simple straightforward mixing method by L-CDs embedded into LCNFs matrix (Fig. 1d). As expected, compared to the LCNFs films without the addition of L-CDs, the L-CDs/LCNFs composite films demonstrated superior UV-blocking performance and sensitive humidity & pH responses (Fig. 1e and Fig. 1f). This strategy of the development for multi-functional L-CDs/LCNFs composite film with UV-blocking and humidity & pH response for food preservation and intelligent monitoring will be comprehensively analyzed and demonstrated below.

Due to the presence of lignin in LCNFs gel, the L-CDs/LCNFs composite films will show a light yellow color, which is not conducive to the optical visual performance. Therefore, colorimetric analysis of the composite films was adopted to evaluate the impact of different parameter conditions on optical characteristics. With the increase of the dilution ratio of L-CDs, the brightness value (L*) of the composite films exhibited an increasing tendency, along with the decreasing tendencies of red-green (a*), yellow-blue (b*) and color difference (ΔE*) values (Fig. 2a). Furthermore, with the increase of the addition of L-CDs ranging from 6 mL to 9 mL (Fig. 2b), the addition of SL ranging from 0.03 g to 0.12 g (Fig. 2c), the colorimetric parameters of different composite films show an opposite trend compared to the above results. The optical photos of composite films with lignin contents of 0 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 % show a slight noticeable differences with the deepening of color (Fig. 2d). Therefore, the dilution ratio of L-CDs, the addition of L-CDs, the addition amount of SL, and the lignin content in LCNFs have important effects on the optical properties of the composite films (Fig. S1), and further fluorescence tests are performed. It was found that the L-CDs/LCNFs composite film prepared under the conditions of the dilution ratio of L-CDs (20 D), the addition of L-CDs (8 mL), the addition amount of SL (0.06 g), and the lignin content of 5 % in LCNFs, which shows the strongest fluorescence properties (Figs. 2f-h, S2). It was reported that the conjugated structures in lignin were characterized by aromatic ring bonds and delocalized π-electron delocalization, which enhanced the fluorescence properties of the composite films [35]. So, the optimum preparation conditions of the composite films have been determined by comprehensive analysis of colorimetric parameters and fluorescence properties. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of preferred L-CDs/LCNFs composite film further proved its superiority (Fig. 2e). The excellent fluorescence properties of the composite film lie in the main role of L-CDs and the auxiliary strengthening effect of adding lignin.

As it is known that lignin is a good UV absorber, the addition of lignin in LCNFs confers the composite films with effective UV-blocking properties. The UV transmittance and the UV absorption of the different L-CDs/LCNFs composite films were assessed within 200 to 600 nm, as presented in Fig. 3a-d. The data show that all composite films with different dilution ratio of L-CDs, different addition amount of L-CDs, and different addition amount of SL show low transparency in all UV regions ranging from C to A. It suggests that lignin and L-CDs can effectively block UV in composite films. The UV-blocking rates for UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A regions in different L-CDs/LCNFs composite films are presented in Table S1. When the dilution ratio of L-CDs was 20 D, the composite film shows the highest UV-blocking rate. Compared with CNFs film, LCNFs film and L-CDs/L-CNFs film show a lower transparency, the higher absorbance, and higher UV-blocking rate (Fig. 3d-e). These phenomena demonstrate that the addition of L-CDs and lignin is conducive to the improvement of UV-blocking properties for nanocellulose composite films.

L-CDs/LCNFs composite films with different addition amount of SL have strong absorption in the ultraviolet region (200–400 nm) (Fig. 3f). The absorption band at ≈250 nm is ascribed to the characteristic absorption band of the benzene ring from lignin in L-CNFs, originating from the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [36,37]. The absorption band at ≈340 nm is ascribed to the characteristic absorption band of oxygen-containing chemical groups from lignin in L-CNFs and L-CDs, originating from the n → π* transition of oxygen-containing –COOH [29]. With the increase of SL content, the characteristic absorption intensity of benzene ring appeared the trend of first increasing and then decreasing indicating that the increase of SL content was conducive to the formation of L-CDs. Although the further increase of SL addition is conducive to the increased L-CDs in the composite films to exhibit higher UV absorption. However, there is no obvious advantage in improving the comprehensive performance of the composite films. Therefore, from the perspective of resource saving and fluorescence quenching of high-concentration carbon quantum dots, the optimal addition amount of SL is 0.06 g.

Mechanical properties are important for evaluating the practical utility of films (Figs. 3g-i, S3). It shows that the addition of L-CDs and lignin could strengthen the mechanical attributes of L-CDs/LCNFs composite films. As the dilution ratio of L-CDs rose to 20 D, the composite film achieved a peak tensile strength of 49.63 MPa, with an elongation at break of 20.53 %. Furthermore, when the L-CDs content was 8 mL, the film's tensile strength reached 53.12 MPa, and the elongation at break to 21.52 %. At the optimal dilution ratio of 20 D and with an optimal 8 mL addition of L-CDs, the amount of SL was further optimized. The mechanical strength of the composite film peaked at 55.65 MPa, and the elongation at break reached an optimal 21.65 %. The reason for the enhancement of mechanical properties of L-CDs may be that a certain amount of hydrophilic L-CDs formed hydrogen bonds with the LCNFs substrate. When the lignin content of L-CNFs was changed, the tensile strength of the composite films increased to the maximum 61.51 MPa and the elongation at break increased to the maximum 26.35 %. The logical explanation might be that a certain amount of hydrophilic L-CDs formed hydrogen bonds to the LCNFs substrate to enhance the mechanical properties of the film [38]. Meanwhile, lignin played a physical cross-linking role between L-CDs and nanocelluloses through surface characteristic groups (-OH, -COOH, etc.), which enhanced the internal connection points of the composite film and thus improved the mechanical strength [39].

The effects of L-CDs and lignin on the microstructure of the L-CDs/LCNFs composite films were further studied (Figs. 4 , S4). Compared with CNFs film, LCNFs film showed a more continuous, smooth and flat surface micro-state without defects. This improvement was attributed to the abundant functional groups of lignin, which formed hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions with CNFs [40,41]. These interactions effectively fill the inter-fiber pores, reduce the surface roughness, and form a homogeneous coating, covering the nanofibers on the surface of the CNFs film to improve the denseness and homogeneity of the films (Fig. 4a-b) [42]. The incorporation of L-CDs disrupts the compact structure of LCNFs films, restoring the nanoscale crack structure. These cracks provide numerous moisture adsorption sites, which are beneficial for achieving and enhancing the humidity-responsive properties of L-CDs/LCNFs composite films (Fig. 4c). SL-derived L-CDs exhibit spherical and monodisperse nanoparticles with the average diameter of 3.2 nm (Figs. 4d, S5). L-CDs in the L-CDs/LCNFs composite film shows a crystal structure with the lattice distance of 0.21 nm, which is similar to the graphite (100) plane [43]. This phenomenon indicates that the L-CDs has a certain graphite-like crystal structure and is embedded in the L-CDs/LCNFs composite film.

The FTIR spectra of CNFs, LCNFs, and L-CDs/LCNFs film are presented in Fig. 5a. It displayed that the chemical structures of L-CDs remained unchanged significantly after embedding in LCNFs. The spectrum shows that the absorption peak observed at 3447 cm−1 was associated with the stretching vibration of OH [44]. It obviously indicated that the strength of the peak at 3443 cm−1 was more evident and shifted to a low wavenumber in the LCNFs film compared to the CNFs film. This phenomenon was mainly due to the presence of lignin, which facilitated the dispersion of CNFs and prevented agglomeration, resulting in more exposed hydroxyl groups and the formation of hydrogen bonding between the lignin and CNFs [45–47]. Additionally, the presence of lignin, containing abundant hydroxyl groups, leads to an enhancement in the intensity of the hydroxyl peaks in the FTIR spectrum of LCNFs film. Compared with LCNFs film, it was apparent that the hydroxyl peak weakened and became narrower of L-CDs/LCNFs film. This phenomenon indicated that the formation of hydrogen bonds within the L-CDs and LCNFs molecules. Besides, it is demonstrative that the CH peak at 2908 cm−1 has a tendency to enhance in the order from CNFs, LCNFs, L-CDs/LCNFs films, which may be due to the addition of lignin and L-CDs introducing CH groups from CH2 and CH3 groups [48]. For LCNFs and L-CDs/LCNFs films, the peak at 1635 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of CO, and the peak at 1502 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of the benzene ring CC in lignin [49]. Compared with LCNFs films, L-CDs/LCNFs films exhibit stronger CO and CC characteristic peaks, which further proves the successful recombination of L-CDs into LCNFs matrix.

Fig. 5b shows the XRD patterns of CNFs, LCNFs and L-CDs/LCNFs films. There are three characteristic peaks of cellulose in CNFs film, which are located at 2θ = 14.5°, 22° and 34°, attributable to the (110), (002) and (040) diffraction planes of cellulose, sequentially [50–52]. Lignin and L-CDs did not contribute new crystal structures, which may be due to the low content of L-CDs and lignin. The results of XRD analysis further confirm the physical interaction between components in L-CDs/LCNFs film. Zeta potential measurements of L-CDs solution, LCNFs film, and L-CDs/LCNFs film were carried out as shown in Fig. 5c. L-CDs showed a positive potential, which echoes the amino (-NH2) and hydroxyl (-OH) groups in L-CDs. These functional groups can release H+ in solution, which leads to the surface of L-CDs to be positively charged. The Zeta potential of LCNFs film was negative, which was mainly attributable to the presence of abundant hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) in lignin and nanocellulose, which can dissociate a negative charge in water. The addition of L-CDs decreased the negative potential of the composite L-CDs/LCNFs film from −48.85 mV to −0.95 mV, which could be due to the formation of the hydrogen bond within L-CDs and LCNFs, which in turn changed the zeta potential.

To further verify the successful fabrication of L-CDs/LCNFs films, XPS surveys were carried out, as shown in Fig. 5d-g and Table S2. L-CDs/LCNFs film was consisted of mainly C (61.26 wt%), O (32.09 wt%), N (2.11 wt%), B (2.01 wt%), S (1.51 wt%), Na (1.02 wt%), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra provide in-depth analysis of chemical groups. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 5d) reveals peaks at 286.8 eV, 285.6 eV, 285.1 eV, 284.8 eV, 284.3 eV, which are attributed to CO, CS, CN, CC, CC, respectively [53,54]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 5e) shows peaks at 531.98 eV, 531.08 eV, 530.48 eV, which are assigned to O=C-O, C-OH, CO, respectively [55,56]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5f) exhibits peaks at 399.28 eV, 398.58 eV, 397.28 eV, which are attributed to NH, CN, CN, correspondingly. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 5g) exhibits peaks at 169.08 eV, 168.18 eV, 167.08 eV, 164.18 eV, which correspond to SO, CS, 1/2P orbitals of -SO3, C-S=O derived from lignin, respectively [57,58]. The molecular interactions of components in L-CDs/LCNFs films are shown in Fig. 5h, mainly the hydrogen bond interactions between L-CDs and LCNFs. The elucidation of S- and N-related chemical groups in FTIR and XPS analyses, as well as surface charge results from zeta potential analysis, confirmed the successful binding of L-CDs into the L-CDs/LCNFs films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humidity and acid-base co-response optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: L-CDs/LCNFs film has good humidity and acid-base intelligent response characteristics (Fig. 6 ). As demonstrated in Fig. 6a-b, the fluorescence intensity of L-CDs/LCNFs composite film attained the maximum peak at pH = 9, and decreased with the increase of alkalinity or acidity, suggesting a good pH response fluorescence characteristics. The pH sensitivity is related to various functional groups (carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups) at the surface of L-CDs. The pH response mechanism of the L-CDs/LCNFs composite film is based on the protonation and deprotonation of the surface chemical groups of L-CDs. In an alkaline environment, the carboxyl and -OH groups of L-CDs may be deprotonated by forming hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of LCNFs, resulting in enhanced blue fluorescence [59]. Under strongly acidic or strongly basic conditions, the possible reason for the greatly reduced emission intensity is that at extreme pH values, strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding is easy to occur between carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups. As a result, the carboxyl and -OH functional groups of L-CDs are easily aggregated, generating a decrease in fluorescence intensity of L-CDs/LCNFs film. The formation, disconnection and reconstruction of hydrogen bonds are crucial for the pH response mechanism of L-CDs in L-CDs/LCNFs film (Fig. 6c). The fluorescence intensity of L-CDs/LCNFs film decreases with the increase of ambient humidity, showing an obvious humidity response (Fig. 6d-e). The reason is that as humidity increases, abundant new hydrogen bonds are formed between the surface functional groups (-COOH, -OH, -NH2) of L-CDs and H2O molecules, resulting in the loss of vibration energy of OH stretching, which reduces the fluorescence intensity of the composite film (Fig. 6f) [60].

Interestingly, it is found that the conductivity, resistance and resistance change rate of L-CDs/LCNFs films are nonlinear responses to the surrounding humidity. In instances where the environmental humidity is exceptionally low, specifically at 20 %, the electron migration between adjacent L-CDs mainly relies on the jump pattern. Only a few electrons with very high energy can complete the transition, reflecting the low conductivity (0.065 mS/m) and high resistance (180 MΩ) of L-CDs/LCNFs film at low humidity. However, when L-CDs/LCNFs film is exposed to an environment with high relative humidity, the oxygen-containing groups (-COOH, -OH) upon the surface of adjacent L-CDs can construct hydrogen bonds with H2O molecules. It will markedly promote the formation and migration of electrons, showing a trend of significant increase in conductivity and decrease in resistance (Fig. 7a-b). When the L-CDs/LCNFs film is exposed to an ambient humidity of 90 %, the conductivity increases significantly to 1.3 mS/m and the resistance decreases to 1.2 MΩ. Moreover, it is easier to induce electron migration between adjacent L-CDs by hydrogen bond generation than by jumping mode [61]. The schematic diagram of electron migration under dry state and wet state are shown in Fig. 7c. Therefore, the conductivity of L-CDs/LCNFs film increases with the increase of humidity, showing a good response to the ambient humidity. Compared with CNFs film and LCNFs film, the lower contact angle on the surface of L-CDs/LCNFs film further confirms the positive correlation between good wettability and wet response performance (Fig. 7d). In addition, the optical photo of circuit composed of L-CDs/LCNFs film under environmental humidity of 95 %, which further shows the excellent electrical conductivity of L-CDs/LCNFs film (Fig. 7e). Thus, L-CDs/LCNFs films show sensitive changes in conductivity and resistance to humidity, which provides a theoretical basis for the wide application in intelligent monitoring.

Exposing food to UV-A and UV-B radiation induces a marked deterioration in its quality and sensory profile [4]. The UV-blocking rate of the L-CDs/LCNF films is presented in Table S1, which blocked 99.86 % of UV-B and 87.90 % of UV-A. As discussed above, the L-CDs/L-CNFs films fabricated in this research showcased out-bound UV-blocking peculiarities. Consequently, coating fresh strawberries, grapes, and pork with L-CDs/CNFs film to prevent oxidative spoilage caused by prolonged UV exposure was attempted to estimate its actual efficacy in preserving food. Fig. 7f shows the changes of food (left without film, right coated with L-CDs/LCNFs film) after exposure to UV light for 0, 12, 24, 36 h. Notably, the non-packaged foods started to turn red in 12 h and darken with time. Under the protection of L-CDs/L-CNFs composite film, pork, cherries and grapes all maintained good freshness compared to those exposed to UV radiation. It indicates that L-CDs/L-CNFs films maybe have potential application prospects in the UV-blocking performance in food resistance to photo-oxidation [62]. In terms of extended applications, L-CDs/LCNFs films have good humidity and pH smart responsiveness in monitoring spoilage during food storage. Specifically, 10 g of fresh pork was placed on a homemade plate coated with L-CDs/CNFs film and then exposed to UV light at 25 °C. During storage, the electrical conductivity and resistance of the sample were monitored every 1 h. Figs. 7g and S6 show the conductivity and resistance changes of pork placed under UV light for 1–8 h. It was found that the electrical conductivity of pork increased and the electrical resistance decreased with the extension of storage time. To further corroborate these observations, supplementary pH and colorimetric analyses were performed. At each UV exposure time point (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, and 14 h), three independent pork samples (10 g) were homogenized with 90 mL of deionized water, centrifuged at 6000 rpm, and their supernatants analyzed. The pH values (the mean of the triplicate measurements) progressively increased from 5.80 (2 h) to 7.20 (14 h) with a visible color shift from light pink to deep red, consistent with slight biochemical degradation (Fig. 7h). The observed phenomena may be attributed to two sequential processes: initial glycolytic degradation of glycogen yielding lactic acid, followed by proteolytic breakdown of proteins into alkaline substances, such as ammonia and amines. These biochemical changes alter pH levels, which modulate ionic strength and electrical conductivity [63–65].

In summary, we have successfully obtained lignosulfonate-derived carbon quantum dots by a simple, fast and efficient microwave method. Thus, the composite films were obtained by doping L-CDs into lignocellulosic nanofibers, showing strong mechanical properties, superior UV-blocking rate, high visible light transmittance and sensitivity to humidity and pH ranging from acidic to alkaline environments. The effects of the different dilution ratios (5 D, 10 D, 20 D, 30 D, 40 D) of L-CDs, the added volumes (6 mL, 8 mL, 10 mL) of L-CDs solution，the added masses (0.03 g, 0.045 g, 0.06 g, 0.09 g, 0.12 g) of SL, and lignin contents (0 %, 2 %, 5 %, 10 %) in lignocellulosic nanofibers on the colorimetric parameters, fluorescence spectra, UV-blocking rates, UV–vis absorption spectra, and mechanical strength of the L-CDs/LCNFs composite films were comprehensively analyzed to obtain the target material. The optimal composite film was obtained when the added masses was 0.06 g, the L-CDs dilution ratio was 20 D, the added volume of L-CDs solution was 8 mL, and the lignin content in the matrix was 5 %. Under these conditions, the composite film demonstrated the preferred fluorescence performance. The optimized film shows a superior UV-blocking rate (99.86 %), good visible light transmittance (85.60 %), and excellent mechanical strength (61.51 MPa). Under the protection of composite film, pork, strawberries and grapes all maintained good freshness compared to those exposed to UV radiation after 36 h. Furthermore, the conductivity of the composite film increases from 0.065 mS/m to 1.30 mS/m exponentially with the increase of humidity from 20 % to 90 %, showing good humidity response monitoring. Utilizing the smart response effect of the composite film to pH, the corruption status of pork during storage can be monitored based on the changes in electrical conductivity and resistance. So, the multifunctional L-CDs/LCNFs composite films show potential applications in food preservation and intelligent monitoring to protect food from UV-induced photo-oxidation and to monitor food spoilage.
